svg.text: TOK PISIN
GRAMMAR — A NEW LANGUAGE
TOK PISIN: NOT BROKEN ENGLISH
Tok Pisin is a fully-fledged language with its own grammar
Native speakers: 2-4M (nativized creole, not pidgin)
Total speakers: ~4-8M including L2 in Papua New Guinea
DUAL PRONOUNS (ENGLISH LACKS THESE)
Tok Pisin has FOUR numbers for pronouns: singular, dual, trial, plural!
mi = I • mitupela = we two • mitripela = we three • mipela = we
yu = you • yutupela = you two • yupela = you (plural)
em = he/she/it • tupela = they two • ol = they
INCLUSIVE vs. EXCLUSIVE "WE"
Tok Pisin distinguishes whether "we" includes the listener:
mipela = "we" (exclusive: I + others, NOT you)
yumi = "we" (inclusive: I + you + others)
English has no equivalent distinction
ASPECT MARKERS (NOT TENSES)
bai: future/irrealis • bin: past/completive • stap: progressive
Em i kaikai stap. "He is eating." (stap = continuous)
Em i bin kaikai. "He ate/has eaten." (bin = past)
ENGLISH GRAMMAR · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
ENGLISH GRAMMAR
SVO word order • no case inflection on nouns • articles
Two tenses (past/non-past): I walk / I walked
Rich tense-aspect system using auxiliaries:
I walk / I walked / I will walk / I am walking / I have walked
ENGLISH PRONOUNS (SIMPLE)
I / you / he-she-it / we / you(pl.) / they
No dual or trial • No inclusive/exclusive "we" distinction
Tok Pisin grammaticalizes distinctions English ignores
GRAMMAR DIVERGENCE FROM ENGLISH
i: predicate marker after subject (no English equivalent)
Em i go. "He goes." (i marks predicate boundary)
This feature from Austronesian substrate (not from English)
Tok Pisin is creole: English vocabulary + Melanesian grammar
PLATE 45c ALT · TOK PISIN / ENGLISH · GRAMMAR
